role_summary: President of the San Francisco NAACP; head pastor of Third Baptist Church of San Francisco, 1976-2025.
biographical_paragraph: Eight students enrolled in the only college seminar Martin Luther King Jr. ever co-taught at Crozer Theological Seminary; Amos C. Brown was one of them [src: 2]. The path that brought him there began in Jackson, Mississippi, where he was born February 20, 1941, and ran through a meeting with King in 1956 at the NAACP's national convention in San Francisco [src: 2]. The year before that meeting, Brown had launched what would become the NAACP's first youth council [src: 2]. A 1961 lunch-counter sit-in in Mississippi put him in jail alongside King, and Brown also rode with the Freedom Rides that year [src: 2]. After a 1964 Morehouse College Bachelor's, he took an M.Div. at Crozer and later a D.Min. at United Theological Seminary [src: 2]; Third Baptist Church of San Francisco was his pulpit from 1976 until June 2025, the long pastorate parallel to his presidency of the city's NAACP branch [src: 2]. The Civil Rights History Project archives his oral history at the Library of Congress as entry 2 [src: 1].

sources.title: Amos C. Brown oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress
